entries.title: Horoscope for Friday, May 01, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon alert</h2><p>There are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions today. The full moon in Scorpio peaks at 12:23 p.m. Chicago time today.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Because your communications with others are grounded and realistic today, not only is this a great day for discussions with others, but it’s also an excellent day for writers who will see creative ways to express themselves.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Financial and business affairs are favored today because you'll be realistic about issues and pay attention to details. Whatever you do might attract money or earnings to you. You're strong now with the sun in your sign, and behind-the-scenes research will pay off.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Today, Venus is in your sign dancing with stern Saturn, which makes you want to live up to your duties and obligations in a relationship. This is an excellent day to sit down with a loved one and talk about what you expect from each other — the real deal.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This is a friendly, sociable week, and today in particular, some of you might explore a secret love affair or a hidden relationship with someone of an age difference. This might involve a boss because your ambitions are obvious right now.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Today you might be attracted to someone older. Or you might act like a guide or a mentor to someone who seeks counsel from you. A love relationship between people of different ages might also occur today. As Arte Johnson used to say, "Verrry interesting."</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You make an excellent impression on parents, bosses and people in authority today because they see you as someone who is willing to accept the duties and obligations that others expect you to fulfill. You have charm and grace today. People respect you.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Today, your ruler Venus is dancing beautifully with Saturn, which will help you study and learn. It will also give you the patience and attention to detail to make elaborate travel arrangements. You might meet someone from another culture who is older or has more authority. An interesting day!</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Financial discussions, as well as talks about wills and inheritances, will go well today because people will be reasonable and willing to deal with practical matters. No one will overlook details. (You stand to benefit.) Meanwhile, romance with someone older could be passionate and memorable.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>This is a solid day to discuss the reality of an emotional relationship or any relationship that is very close to you. For example, you will find it easier than usual to sit down and arrive at an understanding of mutual expectations. Define some boundaries. Speak up about what you want.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>It will be easy to function in your job today because co-workers will be helpful. Not only will your work flow easily, but you might also see ways to improve the appearance of your workplace. For some, a work-related romance — especially with a boss or someone older — might begin.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>Romance is in the stars today! In fact, romance with someone of an age difference is likely. Nevertheless, all romantic discussions will deal with the practical aspects of the relationship — time, place, cost, shared responsibilities, shared expenses, that sort of thing. These are all real considerations.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p>You might redecorate where you live today in a way that shows a commitment to where you are living. Whatever you do, you will do so with great care and discipline and be thorough about procedures and details. You know what you want.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actor and musician Jamie Dornan (1982) shares your birthday. You're tenacious, compassionate and nurturing. You have acute powers of intellectual perception. Take time this year for reflection. You might find yourself renewing your interest in spiritual or religious beliefs. Look at your past experiences so you don't repeat mistakes. Explore philosophies that give you better self-awareness.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Policing grows more dangerous as laws change and public sentiment shifts
entries.summary: <p>Two Chicago <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/28/chicago-john-bartholomew-john-lyke-alphanso-talley-swedish-chicago-police-shooting" target="_blank" >police officers</a> were shot April 25 inside Endeavor Health Swedish Hospital. One officer, 38-year-old John Bartholomew, was killed. The other, a 57-year-old, is fighting for his life. This happened inside a hospital during a routine prisoner transport. And once again, people in Illinois are acting shocked, as if this came out of nowhere.</p><p>It did not. This was predictable and preventable. The suspect’s history proves it.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://cwbchicago.com/2026/04/7-time-felon-escaped-from-electronic-monitoring-killed-a-chicago-police-officer-and-gravely-wounded-a-second-cop-officials.html" target="_blank" >background</a> of the man charged in the shootings, Alphanso Talley, is a case study in systemic failure.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-530000" name="module-530000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>The man has a long criminal record. He has been arrested multiple times going back to 2017; some arrests led to prison sentences. Still, he was released on electronic monitoring in December 2025 even though he had a history of failing to show up to court.</p><p>This is not an anomaly. The crime-reporting website CWB Chicago <a class="Link" href="https://cwbchicago.com/2026/04/7-time-felon-escaped-from-electronic-monitoring-killed-a-chicago-police-officer-and-gravely-wounded-a-second-cop-officials.html" target="_blank" >reports</a> Talley is the fifth person charged this year, while on felony pretrial release, with killing or trying to kill someone in Chicago.</p><p>This is the justice system operating with a revolving door. A cycle of release without accountability. A structure that prioritizes second, third, fourth and fifth chances for offenders while police officers get no second chances.</p><h3>National crisis</h3><p>This is exactly what I warned about in an <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/9/25/23889180/attacks-on-police-officers-trends-lawlessness-cash-bond-justice-accountability-tom-weitzel-op-ed" target="_blank" >opinion essay</a> for the Chicago Sun-Times in 2023. I wrote that Illinois and the nation were entering a dangerous era of lawlessness and collapsing accountability. I wrote that attacks on police officers were rising. I wrote that violent offenders were being cycled through the system with no meaningful consequences.</p><p>Nothing has changed. The numbers prove it.</p><p>The national picture is a crisis.</p><p>This year, as of April 1, more than 82 police officers nationwide have been shot, according to the <a class="Link" href="https://national.fop.net/shotkilled412026#page=1" target="_blank" >National Fraternal Order of Police</a>. Ambush attacks continue to rise, with offenders deliberately targeting officers without warning.</p><p>These are not random events. They are the direct result of a culture that has spent years undermining law enforcement and, in some cases, empowering offenders.</p><p>Being shot should never be considered part of the job. Being killed is not part of the job. No officer signs up to be executed in a hospital hallway by a repeat offender who should never have been on the street.</p><p>Why does this keep happening? Because the system continues to enable it by allowing the following:<br></p><ol id="rte-462e54b1-44ef-11f1-9c65-33512ed4360e" start="1"><li>Laws that restrict proactive policing and remove the tools officers need. Limits on proven tools — such as investigative stops, vehicle pursuits or discretionary enforcement — curb proactive policing. When statutes raise thresholds so high that officers can’t intervene until after a crime occurs, proactive work disappears.</li><li>The Illinois SAFE-T-Act reshaped pretrial detention and changed detention thresholds for violent offenders. It changed<a class="Link" href="https://www.illinoiscourts.gov/news/1270/illinois-supreme-court-issues-order-allowing-pretrial-hearings-under-the-safet-act-to-be-remote-when-necessary/news-detail/" target="_blank" > timelines</a> for detention hearings, brought about <a class="Link" href="https://www.isba.org/sections/bench/newsletter/2025/03/whatisamotionforrelief" target="_blank" >tougher evidentiary requirements</a> for prosecutors and added administrative burdens for police officers. </li><li>The court system in Cook County has released people accused of attacking police. This happened recently in <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagotribune.com/2026/04/13/man-charged-with-resisting-arrest-and-battery-of-2-orland-park-police-officers/?share=ameiy4aa3ehtpsgr2e2m" target="_blank" >Orland Park</a>, where a man accused of punching and kicking officers in the face was released with GPS monitoring.</li><li>Numerous electronic monitoring failures that endanger people. Violations sometimes don’t get enforced, or alerts aren’t acted on. High-risk offenders are sometimes placed on GPS monitoring rather than being detained, which was the case for the accused shooter at Swedish Hospital. </li><li>Public rhetoric that paints officers as villains and criminals as victims.</li><li>Voices in the media that hammer law enforcement, fueling hostility and chaos. </li></ol><p>The human cost is unbearable. Officer John Bartholomew served for 10 years. The critically injured officer has served for 21 years. These are not statistics. These are fathers, husbands, partners, colleagues. Their families now face a lifetime of grief because the system that was supposed to protect them failed.</p><p>Nothing changes because no one in power confronts reality.</p><p>My 2023 opinion essay laid out the warning signs. It was headlined: “Attacks on police officers are rising. The lawlessness must end.” We are exactly where I feared we would be: more officers shot, more officers killed, more offenders emboldened and more communities destabilized.</p><p>Illinois cannot keep pretending this is normal. It cannot keep pretending this is acceptable. And it cannot keep pretending that weakening law enforcement somehow strengthens justice.</p><p>When you weaken law enforcement, you weaken public safety. The people who pay the price include the officers who run toward danger while many others debate talking points.</p><p><i>Tom Weitzel retired as chief of the Riverside Police Department in May 2020 after 37 years in law enforcement.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-d20000" name="module-d20000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Tom Weitzel

entries.title: Great Lakes clean-up targets plastic pellets polluting waterways
entries.summary: <p>This weekend, thousands of volunteers will descend on Great Lakes beaches as part of the second annual International Pellet Count. Preproduction pellets, also known as nurdles, are the polystyrene feedstock used in manufacturing plastic products. About the size of a grain of cooked rice, nurdles are made from fossil fuels and a laundry list of harmful chemicals, including phthalates, Bisphenol A, or BPA, flame retardants, heavy metals and polyfluoroalkyl substances, or, PFAS. </p><p>The raw material eventually devolves into microplastic pollution. Nurdles also attract, concentrate and transport harmful chemicals within the environment, where they can leach out and enter the food chain.</p><p>To illustrate the extent of the problem, volunteers across America, with a particular focus here in the Great Lakes, are hosting nurdle counts and clean-up events at their local waterways Saturday. The goal is to help identify plastic pellet pollution and make the case for action.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-280000" name="module-280000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>It is long past time that we demand accountability from those in the plastics industry that are carelessly transporting nurdles without appropriate safeguards. As recently as January, a tanker truck in Michigan skipped the manufacturing middleman and crashed in a blizzard. The ruptured tanker <a class="Link" href="https://www.mlive.com/environment/2026/02/semi-trailer-spilled-plastic-nurdle-pellets-into-kalamazoo-river.html" target="_blank" >spilled several thousand pounds of nurdles</a> on the highway and into the Kalamazoo River. The spill is less than four miles upriver from where the Kalamazoo feeds into Lake Michigan.</p><p>Disturbingly, this was not an isolated incident.</p><p>“We’ve had those spills before on the highway — not uncommon,” Saugatuck Township Fire Chief Greg Janik told an MLive reporter.</p><p>A <a class="Link" href="https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969720347562" target="_blank" >2018 University of Western Ontario study</a> looked at 66 beaches in each Great Lakes state and Ontario and found 42 beaches contaminated with nurdle pellets. Preproduction nurdle pellets were the most common piece of plastic pollution collected by volunteers in <a class="Link" href="https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10661-025-14354-2" target="_blank" >a 2025 global coastal survey</a>.</p><h3>Polluting with impunity</h3><p>The cavalier attitude the plastics industry has toward handling nurdles and their adverse impact on the environment underscores the free pass they have to pollute with impunity. If these pellets were in liquid form, they would be considered an oil or chemical spill, and industry would be held accountable. Due to regulatory loopholes, the industry is not held properly accountable for the toxic pollutants from nurdles that end up in our waterways.</p><p>Thankfully, some lawmakers are waking up to this chronic problem. At the national level, U.S. Sen. Richard Durbin, D-Illinois, and U.S. Rep. Mike Levin, D-California, have introduced the <a class="Link" href="https://www.durbin.senate.gov/newsroom/press-releases/durbin-introduces-legislation-to-stymie-plastic-pollution-in-waterways" target="_blank" >Plastic Pellet Free Waters Act</a>. The bill would require the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency to use its existing authority under the Clean Water Act to prohibit the discharge of plastic pellets and other pre-production plastic into waterways. </p><p>In Illinois, House Rep. Joyce Mason and state Sen. Julie Morrison introduced a <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus?DocNum=3278&GAID=18&DocTypeID=HB&LegId=161766&SessionID=114" target="_blank" >bill</a> similar to Durbin’s, instructing the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency to adopt rules to regulate preproduction plastic pellets. While in Michigan, where the latest spill occurred, a pair of bills aim to monitor drinking water for the presence of microplastics while deploying scientific tools and strategies to keep our drinking water free from toxic plastic pollution.</p><p>It’s been widely reported that 86% of the litter the Alliance’s Adopt-a-Beach volunteers pick up from Great Lakes beaches is either fully or partially plastic. These plastics don’t break down in the environment. They only get smaller and smaller. </p><p>Microplastics are now found in the water in all the Great Lakes, as well as in fish, birds and humans. There is mounting scientific evidence of the devastating impacts plastic pollution has on the Great Lakes from drinking water to aquatic life. The threat is so severe that scientists issued an urgent call for action in the<a class="Link" href="https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(23)02198-0/abstract" target="_blank" > Lancet last summer</a> to protect human health.</p><p>People who love the Great Lakes are taking notice, too. A recent poll from <a class="Link" href="https://usa.oceana.org/reports/nearly-8-in-10-u-s-voters-support-national-policies-that-reduce-single-use-plastic-foam/" target="_blank" >Oceana</a> revealed an overwhelming number of voters want action. Eighty-two percent of U.S. voters support reducing the amount of single-use plastic state and federal governments buy and use. Eighty percent of U.S. voters support state and local policies that reduce single-use plastic foam.</p><p>Instead of relying on dedicated volunteers to do their work for them, the ultimate solution would be for the plastic industry to be accountable for the pollution it produces in its pursuit of profits. But if plastic companies refuse to take responsibility for protecting our health and our Great Lakes, the only recourse is stricter regulation at the state and federal levels.</p><p><i>Andrea Densham is director of regional government affairs for the Alliance for the Great Lakes, a nonpartisan nonprofit.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-240000" name="module-240000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Andrea Densham

entries.title: Real king schools wannabe god-emperor
entries.summary: <p>I've always been both a small-r republican and an Anglophile, so I looked upon the British Monarchy with at least a pinch of smug superiority. <i>The Windsors are revered for nothing more than birth — not talent, nor accomplishments, nor virtue. It's all a throwback to an earlier stage of human development, when people were not mature enough for representative democracy.</i></p><p>I wasn't feeling smug as I watched King Charles III address Congress the other day. Quite the opposite. Though I remain a small-r republican, I couldn't help but be embarrassed at what we supposedly mature, self-governing Americans have wrought.</p><p>Seated behind Charles were the vice president and speaker of the House, among the highest officers of the land. But befitting our descent from mature self-government, they were both bedecked in the MAGA uniform — blue suits, white shirts and red ties. Everything about them — every word, every gesture and even their clothing — attests to their fealty to their liege lord. Ditto for more than half of the representatives and senators in attendance. While they didn't all wear the uniform (they weren't on camera), the Republicans in Congress have surrendered their self-respect and independent judgment to the man who would be king. They are far more subservient to Donald Trump than any member of Parliament is to Charles.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-620000" name="module-620000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>Trump revels in the servility of his vassals. The White House tweeted a picture of the two men with the caption "two kings." Yes, this tweet is partly a troll to enrage the "libs," but frankly, it doesn't go far enough. Trump aspires not just to be a king but to be a god-emperor. Look around — the massive Trump banners defacing official buildings, the renaming of the Kennedy Center and U.S. Institute of Peace, the coins, the proposed triumphal arch, the Trump ballroom, the reflecting pool (which will now look like Mar-a-Lago north), the new passports, the Trump class battleships, the national park passes, to say nothing of sidelining Congress, disregarding court orders, making unilateral war and attempting to criminally prosecute his critics — all of it flagrantly un-American. And this from the party that garlands itself in flags and showy patriotism.</p><p>Charles had a tricky task. His government (did you notice the way he referred to Keir Starmer as "my" prime minister?) and our other European allies are both horrified and scared about what Trump is doing to the transatlantic relationship. They've been adopting different tactics -— concessions, flattery, toughness — to deal with the unhinged president who has stooped even to threatening military action against a NATO ally. Are the British questioning the "special relationship" with the United States? Oh, yeah. In contrast to Charles' soothing words, the Financial Times reports that Britain's ambassador in Washington, Sir Christian Turner, told a visiting group of students in early February that the phrase "special relationship" was "quite nostalgic, quite backward looking" and that the only country that currently enjoys a special relationship with the U.S. is "probably Israel." He did add that "there is a deep affinity between us, particularly on defense and security," but the message was clear. Trump has strained, if not quite ruptured, relations.</p><p>The king's visit can thus be interpreted as one more effort by the spurned Europeans to keep Trump on their side. Starmer aware of Trump's imperial pretensions, played the "King Charles" card. The pomp, the state visit, the photo ops and the speech to a joint session of Congress are all in service of both flattering and taming the disordered president.</p><p>Charles, despite his title, is not free to say what he thinks. And yet he managed, subtly, to convey his awareness of the threat Trump poses not just to the relationship with the United Kingdom but to the values on which the Euro-American relationship is based. Referring to the shared legal traditions of the U.S. and Great Britain, he said: "The U.S. Supreme Court Historical Society has calculated that Magna Carta is cited in at least 160 Supreme Court cases since 1789, not least as the foundation of the principle that executive power is subject to checks and balances."</p><p>The king also mentioned Sept.11, and reminded his audience that it was the first and only time that NATO invoked Article V. The allies came to our aid, not the reverse — a pointed rebuke to the president who has repeatedly disparaged Europeans' contributions to NATO and to our wars in Iraq and Afghanistan. He then added, "Today, Mr. Speaker, that same, unyielding resolve is needed for the defense of Ukraine and her most courageous people."</p><p>The king's speech closed with an invocation of Abraham Lincoln (hard to top), and a prayer:<br>"I pray with all my heart that our alliance will continue to defend our shared values, with our partners in Europe and the Commonwealth, and across the world, and that we ignore the clarion calls to become ever more inward-looking."</p><p>We all know who is issuing those clarion calls. One of the callers hosted the state dinner, and another was sitting over his right shoulder. The actual king has never looked better than in contrast to the American pretender to a throne.</p><p><i>Mona Charen is policy editor of The Bulwark and host of the “Beg to Differ” podcast.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a30000" name="module-a30000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Mona Charen

entries.title: Apology, thanks for a stranger who helped during Swedish Hospital shooting
entries.summary: <p>On Saturday morning, I consciously decided to deviate from my typical traffic route when running weekend errands. In doing so, I arrived at the intersection of California and Foster avenues, directly across from Swedish Hospital, just as the emergency response to the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/27/suspect-charged-in-swedish-hospital-shooting-that-left-1-cop-dead-and-another-fighting-for-his-life-source" >shooting of two Chicago police officers</a> commenced. Emergency vehicles were arriving from all directions, and a man, who I believe was a civilian, jumped into action by assisting with traffic control. </p><p>At the time, it was unclear to me what was happening. Regrettably, words were exchanged between this gentleman and me as he apparently believed I was attempting to either hit him with my car or circumvent his efforts. Neither was my intention. </p><p>On the off-chance this individual happens to read the Sun-Times, please accept both my apology and gratitude for your valiant effort in assisting as vehicles and pedestrians traversed the intersection.</p><p><i>Jeffrey L. Wiseman, Sauganash</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-4a0000" name="module-4a0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>CTA dropped ball in handling derailment </h3><p>My wife and I were among the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/04/24/commuters-sound-off-on-iffy-cta-after-yellow-line-derailment-during-thursday-evening-rush-hour" >commuters riding</a> the Yellow Line train that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2026/04/23/no-injuries-in-yellow-line-derailment" >derailed outside the CTA’s Howard Street</a> station last week. We are extremely grateful to the compassionate Chicago Fire Department personnel who patiently got us off the train to safety.</p><p>Unfortunately, I can’t say the same for the Chicago Transit Authority. When the train went off the rails, about a dozen employees gathered nearby but seemingly had no idea what to do. Worse, given that we were sitting in a now un-air-conditioned train for the better part of an hour on a hot day, one employee argued when we opened the train doors for air. Thankfully, he relented.</p><p>Once we got to the ground, there was total confusion. When we asked at least three bus drivers if they were driving the Yellow Line shuttle, we were told only, “I’m waiting for instructions.”</p><p>I believe CTA management failed us. Given the time of day and the centrality of the station to the CTA’s system, there should have been senior leadership on the scene to direct employees. There was almost no communication to employees or riders. There should have been a plan.</p><p>We are very grateful that no one was injured. We just wish the CTA had been better prepared. CTA leaders should use this scenario for future training.</p><p><i>James McGowan, Skokie</i></p><h3>Relatable content</h3><p>Reader Scott T. Thompson's thoughts in his <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/letters-to-the-editor/2026/04/25/walterburnett-cha-jesus-hegseth-celiac-disease-rentals-cta-illini-businesses-bears-des-plaines" target="_blank" >recent letter</a> in the Sun-Times' — "Time flies in Retirement" — are refreshingly insightful. Age, particularly longevity, does seem to impact one's perception of time. Nevertheless, while Thompson wonders in retirement "how to make the most of the day" this should be an everyday question for the employed, unemployed and retirees alike. I would also like to commend the Sun-Times for publishing reader content driven by human perspectives that are untethered to political viewpoints or civic controversies.</p><p><i>Robert M. Birndorf, Streeterville</i></p><h3>Kimmel’s comments no joke</h3><p>Jimmy Kimmel's <a class="Link" href="https://time.com/article/2026/04/28/jimmy-kimmel-melania-trump-widow-joke-fired-response/" target="_blank" >comments</a> about Melania having “a glow like an expectant widow” are beyond inappropriate. They are hateful. I doubt they were ad-libbed. Why didn't ABC review Kimmel's material? I am not a Donald Trump fan, but this was just wrong. At the very least, Kimmel needs to apologize to Melania Trump and the president for the so-called joke.</p><p><i>Barb Marion, Orland Park</i></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Dear Abby: Mom has taken the blame for over a decade
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My husband and I have been separated for 15 years. Our two grown children are on the spectrum. For the past two years, our daughter has lived with her father because she wouldn't agree to be part of the team at my home and abide by the basic rules: Pick up after yourself, take your animal out, flush the toilet, etc.</p><p>Now Dad has a girlfriend, and he won't spend any time with his daughter alone without his girlfriend. He also blames me because his son doesn't want to have anything to do with him. Our son refuses to see him because his father beat him when he was 12. My son is 25 now, but he also blames me for what happened. Please help me understand how I can help bridge this gap. — <i>CHALLENGED MOM IN PENNSYLVANIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR CHALLENGED MOM:</b> You cannot bridge a gap you didn't create. You can, however, stay out of the line of fire. Because someone is on the spectrum does not mean they are unable to function. Your daughter will have to accept the rules of the house she lives in, and, if that means she, her father and the girlfriend are a family of three, she'll have to learn to accept it.</p><p>As for your son, you are not to blame for a beating your estranged husband administered when the boy was 12. If your son is still living with you, stand up for yourself and tell him that if he can't behave respectfully, he will have to pack his bags and go. (At that point you should start living your own life.)</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have been a shuttle driver for 10 years. One customer has been somewhat of a regular, together with his wife, for about four years. I have provided quality service during all that time. His wife has graciously thanked me for the rides and often tells me how much they value my service.</p><p>I have sometimes driven them to the airport and home in dangerous driving conditions. He is retired, and his wife works from home as a lawyer. They have a second home in warmer weather. They obviously are not hurting for money. Unfortunately, the husband is the one who pays the fare, and, when he does, he gives me a $2 tip. That's a whopping 3.17% tip on a $63 fare.</p><p>I consider anything under 10% cheap and insulting unless the customer can't afford even that much, or any amount, which sometimes happens. I don't believe in pressuring my customers for tips, so I haven't mentioned it to either of them. My average tip is about 15%, and I often receive more than 25%. How would you deal with this? — <i>DRIVING AN EASY BARGAIN</i></p><p><b>DEAR DRIVING:</b> Let me phrase it this way: I would remember that I was in a service business, dealing with many kinds of people — some more generous than others. Then I would try to decide whether I wanted to be as available to the stingy ones.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>To order "How to Write Letters for All Occasions," send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds), to: Dear Abby — Letter Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: When immigration agents arrested a Chicago mother, a stranger stepped in to care for her 3 kids
entries.summary: <p>When the 17-year-old let Isabel Conde visit, he and his sisters had been on their own for nearly four weeks.</p><p>The boy and the girls, ages 14 and 6, had been fending for themselves in the family’s Southwest Side apartment since immigration agents arrested their mother and moved her to a detention center in Kentucky.</p><p>“What I noticed right away was the girls huddled up together in the door frame of one of the bedrooms,” Conde says, recalling their embrace at the sight of an adult stranger in the home.</p><p>“I wanted to get all the basic facts,” Conde says.</p><p>“Financially, what was going to happen with their apartment? Medically, did they have health insurance? And, legally, what was going on with the mom’s case?”</p><p>During President Donald Trump’s second term, immigration agents have <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/01/02/operation-midway-blitz-parents-children-immigration-detention-mental-health?utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=01052025%20Morning%20Edition&utm_content=01052025%20Morning%20Edition+CID_a6896837d6b9407a2c69df6cd912901d&utm_source=cst_campaign_monitor&utm_term=Trump%20deportation%20campaign%20will%20leave%20lasting%20mark%20on%20kids%20of%20detained%20parents%20experts%20say&tpcc=cst_cm" target="_blank" >taken away the parents</a> of thousands of U.S.-born children. Some of those kids, who are citizens, have moved in with relatives or family friends. Others have landed with strangers or languished in shelters.</p><p>The people who have opened their homes to care for these children often don’t know how long they'll be needed or what emotional fallout they’ll face when the kids leave.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-bd0000" name="image-bd0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5efc8cc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3908x2193+0+207/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F9a%2Fa0aa6cdb46838eecd59920afe595%2Fbroadviewfollow-092425-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5deb3ac/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3908x2193+0+207/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F9a%2Fa0aa6cdb46838eecd59920afe595%2Fbroadviewfollow-092425-01.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1f1c099/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3908x2193+0+207/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F9a%2Fa0aa6cdb46838eecd59920afe595%2Fbroadviewfollow-092425-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Federal agents in an unmarked SUV chat with protesters from opposite sides of a fence around the ICE facility in Broadview, Tuesday, Sept. 23, 2025. | Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1f1c099/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3908x2193+0+207/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F9a%2Fa0aa6cdb46838eecd59920afe595%2Fbroadviewfollow-092425-01.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7711b3d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3908x2193+0+207/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F9a%2Fa0aa6cdb46838eecd59920afe595%2Fbroadviewfollow-092425-01.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1f1c099/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3908x2193+0+207/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F40%2F9a%2Fa0aa6cdb46838eecd59920afe595%2Fbroadviewfollow-092425-01.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A federal immigration agent sits in an SUV near the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility in Broadview last September. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Conde, 27, heard the kids were living alone from a relative who came from the mother’s hometown in Puebla, Mexico.</p><p>The mother, 37, had entered the United States as a teenager in 2006.</p><p>All three of her children were born in Chicago. She worked in a factory and raised them on her own in Chicago Lawn.</p><p>The mother had <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/04/17/ice-operation-midway-blitz-response-illinois-congressional-delegation-letter" target="_blank" >no criminal record</a>, but the immigration agents arrested her on Oct. 12, near their home. WBEZ agreed not to name the woman or her children to protect their safety.</p><p>When Conde visited the apartment weeks later, she started a list of all the adults in the kids’ lives — the people who might be able to take them in.</p><p>She learned the father of the two teens had returned to Mexico. The 6-year-old’s father remained in Chicago, but Conde didn’t think he was “able to care for her.”</p><p>Conde approached the kids’ aunts and uncles. She tried neighbors and their godparents. “No one was able to take all three of them,” she says.</p><p>Conde had worked for immigrant children as a paralegal. So she knew it was only a matter of time before someone called in the Illinois Department of Children and Family Services. The kids could end up in foster care or a shelter. They could be split apart.</p><p>A few nights after meeting the kids, Conde got a call from them.</p><p>“They were in a park and they shared with me that they left their home and were afraid because the father of the youngest was telling them he was going to separate them,” she says.</p><p>Conde was with her own mother: “We got in the car and drove immediately to that park.”</p><p>It was dark. The kids were pulling a wagon, loaded with clothes and a blanket. They were planning to sleep outside. The 17-year-old was pacing.</p><p>“They were scared,” Conde says.</p><p>Conde’s home, a Little Village apartment, had just two bedrooms. She decided it was big enough for all of them.</p><p>But she didn’t want to be accused of kidnapping. She wanted it known that she was taking in the kids for their safety.</p><p>So Conde and her mom packed them into the car and drove to the nearest police station.</p><p>When Conde got there, the father of the 6-year-old was already there. He wanted to take her, but the girl wanted to stay with her sister and brother.</p><p>“The police said we could figure this out between us or they could call the state,” Conde says, referring to that Illinois child welfare agency.</p><p>It took an hour but Conde, speaking Spanish, reached an agreement with the father in front of the officers: She would keep the children together and he could talk to his daughter on the phone whenever he wanted and arrange visits.</p><p>“I was able to take the kids to my house that night,” Conde says.</p><p>Conde’s own father had two extra beds — a queen and a twin. He brought them over and spread them across the living room, forming a “megabed” along with her couch, she says.</p><p>Conde brought in the kids’ wagon, full of clothes, and started a load of laundry.</p><p>She thought she would shelter the kids for just a few days, long enough to identify an adult who was willing and able to take them in.</p><p>Conde also took charge of getting their mother out of immigration detention.</p><p>The family had already spent thousands of dollars on a lawyer whose main advice was asking the mother to sign papers for a “voluntary” deportation, Conde says.</p><p>To Conde, that was a nonstarter. When she managed to reach the mother by phone, she says, “the first thing I told her was, ‘Do not sign anything. You have rights.’”</p><p>Conde found a pro-bono attorney.</p><p>But an immigration judge refused to release the mother on bond. So the attorney turned to the federal court system.</p><p>The legal battles stretched over weeks, then months.</p><p>Conde did her best for the kids.</p><p>“I would drive them to and from school every day,” she says, and would make sure they received the academic and social services they needed.</p><p>“Once we got temporary guardianship paperwork signed by their mom, I was able to start making sure that all of the medical needs were being met,” she says. “Then I got them into mental health therapy, each of them, because they had experienced a severe trauma.”</p><p>“My mom would come several nights a week and help me care for the kids,” Conde says. “She’s the art teacher at Ravenswood Elementary School, and they held a fundraiser for us. And I also tapped into community networks and mutual aid funds.”</p><p>With all the help, Conde could pay the rent on the family’s apartment, so it would be there if the kids’ mother were released.</p><p>“We took them to the ZooLights, the trampoline park, the children’s museum, the aquarium,” Conde says.</p><p>“The youngest grew out of her clothes right away,” Conde says. “She just hit a growth spurt. We had to get new clothes for everybody.”</p><p>“They had tamales with us at Thanksgiving, gifts for Christmas to open,” she says. “We just really made them a part of our family.”</p><p>But Conde was under increasing stress. Less than two weeks into the new year, she started graduate school. It wasn’t easy with the three children in her apartment.</p><p>“I would pray every night and ask that these children could be reunited with their mother,” she says.</p><p>On January 27, two-and-a-half months after Conde first took in the kids, a federal judge in Kentucky ruled the mother’s detention was unlawful. He ordered her immediate release.</p><p>The attorney arranged for volunteers in Kentucky to drive the mother to a spot in Indiana that Conde could reach.</p><p>“I immediately rented a van and picked up the kids from school and was like, ‘Guys, let’s go get your mom!’” Conde says.</p><p>The family was finally reunited.</p><p>Conde drove them back to her apartment. Her own folks came over and made the mother pozole, a Mexican stew.</p><p>“We gave her fresh clothes and then she just fell asleep with her kids in my living room,” Conde says.</p><p>The mother and kids stayed at Conde’s place a few days before returning to their home. It was ready for them because Conde had raised funds to pay the rent since October.</p><p>“They were back in their home and back together,” she says.</p><p>But Conde’s apartment suddenly felt empty.</p><p>“I loved them, and it was the goal all along to reunite them with their mom, but I missed them because we were all like a family for a while,” Conde says, her voice wavering. “I was really sad.”</p><p>This spring, the 17-year-old is graduating from high school. The 14-year-old is having a quinceañera. The younger girl is finishing kindergarten.</p><p>The mother’s immigration case remains pending but the next hearing has been postponed to 2028, giving her time to plan for any outcome. In the meantime, she has qualified for a work permit.</p><p>Conde has wrapped up her first semester of grad school. She’s looking forward to summer classes.</p><p>She gets to see the kids every now and then, but she still feels the loss.</p><p>“After protecting them for so long, I wanted to just keep being with them all the time,” Conde says. “But I knew that it was just the way of things and the way of love.”</p><p>She had to let them go.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/12/19/border-control-body-cameras-videos-midway-blitz-albany-park" target="_blank" >Border Patrol agent’s camera captures detainee’s lament: ‘We don’t have the opportunity that you have’</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/11/28/border-patrol-gregory-bovino-cole-sheridan-broadview-ice" target="_blank" >‘His word means nothing’: Dismissed case reveals dishonesty of Border Patrol commander, protester says</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2025/09/30/transcript-audio-gregory-bovino-arrestees-downtown-chicago-chosen-how-they-look" target="_blank" >Gregory Bovino says arrestees in Downtown Chicago chosen based partly on ‘how they look’</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Chip Mitchell | WBEZ

entries.title: Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson’s budget director pays up after getting years of illegal property tax breaks
entries.summary: <p>Two months after a Chicago Sun-Times Watchdogs investigation revealed that Mayor Brandon Johnson’s city budget director Annette Guzman had taken <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/20/annette-guzman-brandon-johnson-property-tax-breaks-kaegi" target="_blank" >illegal property tax breaks for five years,</a> she's paying back the rest of what she should have paid.</p><p>The Sun-Times reported in May that Guzman had taken property tax breaks she wasn't entitled to for the past five years, saving her $3,432 in taxes on a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/south-loop" target="_blank" >South Loop</a> condo she leases to tenants.</p><p>Annette Guzman got the approval she needed to claim those tax breaks from Cook County Assessor Fritz Kaegi, her former boss.</p><p>After being asked why he'd approved those tax breaks, Kaegi decided Guzman should pay up for only a portion of what she'd failed to pay: $2,071.29, covering only the past three years, out of the five years for which she'd gotten the illegal tax breaks.</p><p>Following the Sun-Times report, records show, Guzman made good on the rest of the property taxes that she should have been paying, the remaining two years. She sent Kaegi two checks on April 3 to cover the additional $1,360.80 she should have paid for 2020 and 2021.</p><p>“Once made aware of the oversight, I took immediate corrective action, including making payment in full to the county,” Guzman said in a written statement issued by the mayor’s office.</p><p>Kaegi’s office also could have made his former employee pay penalties and interest but didn’t.</p><p>But his staff has said the assessor’s office takes responsibility, saying it failed to update Guzman’s assessment file after she moved out of the condo in 2019 to her then-husband’s home on the Northwest Side.</p><p>Guzman now lives in a condo in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bronzeville" >Bronzeville</a>, claiming a homeowner’s exemption there. Under Illinois law, that property tax break is allowed for an owner-occupied property for a person's primary residence. </p><p>But she also was getting the same tax break on the South Loop condo even though she hadn't lived there for seven years. </p><p>Guzman has said she didn’t realized that the assessor kept giving her the tax breaks on the South Loop condo. She gave two reasons for not knowing, saying her lender paid the property taxes with money from her escrow account and that she never changed the mailing address on those tax bills, which still get mailed to her ex-husband’s house four years after they divorced.</p><p>Guzman bought the South Loop condo in 2007. She was living there in December 2018 when Kaegi appointed her deputy assessor, a job that had her overseeing thousands of homeowner exemptions.</p><p>She left Kaegi’s staff in January 2020 to take a job as budget director for Cook County Board President Toni Preckwinkle, then left that post in May 2023 when Johnson made her the city of Chicago budget director.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/03/20/annette-guzman-brandon-johnson-property-tax-breaks-kaegi" >Brandon Johnson’s budget director got illegal property tax breaks for 5 years</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2024/11/15/senior-freeze-property-tax-fritz-kaegi-cook-county-assessor" >Fritz Kaegi OK’d tax breaks for ‘low-income’ seniors, now demands proof they qualify amid Sun-Times probe</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/8/6/22611906/walter-burnett-darlena-williams-burnett-homeowner-property-tax-exemption" >Ald. Walter Burnett, wife wrongly claimed 2 homeowner tax exemptions for 8 years</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/01/17/senior-freeze-property-tax-exemption-fritz-kaegi-armando-saleh-cook-county-assessor-jill-fitzgerald" >City Hall lobbyist pays $96,000 for tax breaks Sun-Times showed he never should have been given</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/12/17/22838399/fritz-kaegi-cook-county-assessor-property-tax-breaks-dead-mobster-joseph-lombardi" >Cook County assessor gave tax breaks to dead mobster — for 6 years</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/10/1/22700932/tammy-duckworth-disabled-veterans-property-tax-exemption-cook-county-assessor-homeowners-pay-nothing" >You pay more in property taxes because 27,288 Cook County homeowners pay nothing</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Tim Novak

entries.title: Chicago emo godfathers American Football returns with first album in seven years
entries.summary: <p>On a recent chilly night in April, a large group of people huddled into a line at the Davis Theater. Everyone was given a blindfold, told to turn off their phones and get comfortable in a dark theater where an image of a blood red sky engulfed the screen. On any other night, it might’ve been the setup for an immersive horror film. But on this night, it was a listening party for American Football’s new album, “LP4,” ahead of its May 1 release.</p><p>“We’ve never done anything like it,” front man Mike Kinsella shared in a recent Zoom chat about the hometown fan event, which also included a Q&A. “I loved the blindfold idea as a kind of sensory deprivation. I know I’d appreciate having time carved out to listen to music intently like that.”</p><p>The dark cocoon was a fitting environment for fully taking in the band’s latest album, its first in seven years and one of its darkest since forming at the University of Illinois in 1997. Songs from opener “Man Overboard” to closer “No Soul to Save” already say it all before the moody guitars, stalking drumbeats and echoey reverb that fills the shadows of the tense subject matter even kick in.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ae0000" name="image-ae0000"></a> <picture data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>American Football’s latest album, “LP4,” is out May 1.</p></figcaption></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>While making the album, two members endured divorces, COVID hit and drummer Steve Lamos submitted a premature resignation, forcing the band into a hiatus. “There was a lot in the mix to write about this time around,” said guitarist Steve Holmes, joining the call from his home in Roscoe Village, just down the street from Kinsella. “There was a long gestation period for this record that started during the pandemic when we were virtually writing and sharing files. And then Lamos quit the band for a while so it was a long stretch where a lot of life happened. When you're an adult and you have kids and there's marriage and divorce, there's a lot more intensity of real-life drama than like teenage feelings of a breakup when you're in college.”</p><p>“But the bigger picture is that we lost the band during COVID,” Kinsella added. “And coming back made us realize the band was also an important part of our lives as we went through the ups and downs.”</p><p>The Midwest emo rock kings have undoubtedly come a long way from those days in Champaign-Urbana, where the music scene with Hum and Braid and the huge support of local label Polyvinyl Records led to the 1999 debut, “LP1.” Even though the record was a one-off and the band fizzled a year later, over time, American Football has gained massive respect as godfathers of a music scene, which inspired their reunion in 2014.</p><p>American Football continues to gain followers and cred from the benchmarks of that early release and its follow-ups, “LP2” and “LP3” (album names still elude them). Phoebe Bridgers is a fan. PinkPantheress sampled the band’s 1999 track “Stay Home” in her hit, “Just for Me.” Turnstile’s Brendan Yates and Wisp’s Natalie Lu have guest spots on “LP4.” Paramore front woman Hayley Williams has also long collaborated with the band — most recently, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/04/22/review-hayley-williams-aragon-ballroom-chicago-show-american-football-mike-kinsella" target="_blank" ><u>pulling Kinsella on stage at Aragon</u></a> during her April solo tour stop.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b40000" name="image-b40000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f4c665a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+212/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F45%2F53fbda84409b8b0f69a693dfd04c%2Fhw-chicago-night-1-digital-37.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9b06af8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+212/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F45%2F53fbda84409b8b0f69a693dfd04c%2Fhw-chicago-night-1-digital-37.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7070277/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+212/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F45%2F53fbda84409b8b0f69a693dfd04c%2Fhw-chicago-night-1-digital-37.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Hayley Williams sings with special guest Mike Kinsella of American Football during her show Tuesday, April 21 at Aragon Ballroom." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7070277/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+212/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F45%2F53fbda84409b8b0f69a693dfd04c%2Fhw-chicago-night-1-digital-37.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4b58d89/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+212/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F45%2F53fbda84409b8b0f69a693dfd04c%2Fhw-chicago-night-1-digital-37.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7070277/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+212/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2F45%2F53fbda84409b8b0f69a693dfd04c%2Fhw-chicago-night-1-digital-37.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Hayley Williams sings with special guest Mike Kinsella of American Football during her show Tuesday, April 21 at Aragon Ballroom.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zachary Gray</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b></b><br>“My daughter's a fan, so I was already going to the show and she reached out,” Kinsella said. “I guess I happen to be like the resident cool old guy.”</p><p>Even though the band members, also including Mike’s cousin Nate Kinsella on bass, may now be middle-aged with college kids of their own, Champaign-Urbana is still a big part of their lives. In 2023, Polyvinyl’s Matt and Darcie Lunsford along with band members and photographer friends Atiba Jefferson and Chris Strong together purchased the iconic “American Football Home.” The property at 704 W. High St. in Urbana makes up the memorable cover art of “LP1” and has become a fan landmark over the years as many take road trips to capture selfies with it in the background. Vans even immortalized the cover art on a pair of shoes in 2025 that sold out instantly.</p><p>“We joked about it for a long time, because it's sort of infiltrated our lives so much,” said Kinsella of the house, which the group has fixed up and now rents out as an Airbnb or offers as a spot for contemporaries to work on albums or crash when they tour through town. In the future, they want to open it up to house shows and are talking with the city of Urbana about a semiannual street festival out in front.</p><p>Beyond the keys to the house, <b></b> the lore of “LP1” continues to directly affect the band to this day, including a 25th anniversary tour of the album in 2024 that was fundamental to the making of their new material, said Kinsella.</p><p>“I didn't realize at the time how much doing that tour did influence the new album. Over the span of that year, we got dialed into the songs and really fleshed them out,” he said, noting the arrangements started to mirror the larger instrumentation and ambient setting they were bringing to the stage on that run. “There was a confluence of bringing what was old, reviving it and then using what we learned to make the new music. … I also think the reason ‘LP1’ resonated was because it was earnest and honest to who we were, and that’s who we still are.”</p><p>Holmes agreed, saying “LP4” finds the band in one of their most positive places yet, listening party vibes be damned. </p><p>“I feel like even though this new record is maybe the darkest lyrically and musically that we’ve put out, it does feel like the most exciting time ever in the band right now,” he said. “We're writing music we're really excited about. Having that hiatus and appreciating what we were missing, there's a newfound love and respect and appreciation for all of each other as a band and as friends and longtime collaborators. And I think you can feel that on this record.”<br></p>
entries.author: Selena Fragassi

entries.title: Des Plaines set to buy most of former Methodist Camp Ground for $4M, ending legal battle
entries.summary: <p>Des Plaines would purchase most of a historic — but flood-prone — campground for $4 million under a deal before the city council.</p><p>If approved, the proposed agreement will end a five-year legal battle over the property at 1250 Campground Road. The <a class="Link" href="https://business.dpchamber.com/list/member/chicago-district-camp-ground-association-inc-des-plaines-5431" target="_blank" >Chicago District Camp Ground Association</a>, the group that owns the eponymous site, already has approved the settlement.</p><p>Founded in 1860 on the Des Plaines River’s east bank, the property long had been called the Methodist Camp Ground but no longer is affiliated with that religious tradition, said the Rev. Daniel Amarei, the association board’s president. The group now is nondenominational Christian, he said.</p><p>Primarily used during summer months, the campground features dozens of cottages, several tabernacles and other structures. <a class="Link" href="https://s3.amazonaws.com/NARAprodstorage/opastorage/live/42/8920/28892042/content/electronic-records/rg-079/NPS_IL/05000429.pdf" target="_blank" >It was added to the National Register of Historic Places</a> in 2005.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260430/religion/des-plaines-set-to-buy-most-of-former-methodist-camp-ground-for-4m-ending-legal-battle/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com</a>.</p>
entries.author: Daily Herald

entries.title: Mayor’s budget director pays up

Isabel Conde couldn’t find adults in their lives to take them in. So she did it herself.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: Mayor Brandon Johnson’s budget director pays up, Lincolnwood Town Center's last days and more news you need to know this May Day.</p><p>📧 <b>Subscribe</b>: Some of those kids, who are citizens, moved in with relatives or friends. Others landed with strangers or languished in shelters. People who open their homes to care for these children often don’t know how long they’ll be needed or what emotional fallout they’ll face when the kids leave.</p><p><b>Stepping in</b>: Three siblings ages 17, 14 and 6 were left to fend for themselves after immigration agents arrested their mother and moved her to a detention center in Kentucky. Isabel Conde heard the kids were living alone and couldn't find adults in their lives to take them in, so she did it herself, for nearly three months. After a federal judge ruled the mother’s detention was unlawful and ordered her immediate release, Conde also took charge of getting her out of detention.</p><p><b>Sharing her story</b>: Conde spoke with WBEZ about her experience as the children's temporary guardian and what's next for their mom's case. </p><p><b>More</b>: </p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-54e0f812-4561-11f1-913e-b71a5f8b86fd"><li>After six months of gathering testimony, video footage and legal records, the Illinois Accountability Commission <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdyuukk-l-i/" target="_blank" >put out its final report Thursday, examining Operation Midway Blitz </a>and alleged abuse carried out by federal agents in and around the city.<br> </li><li>Through her nonprofit Mano a Mano Unidos, Oak Brook resident Patricia Gamboa is <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdyuukk-l-d/" target="_blank" >supporting immigrant families still living in fear after Midway Blitz</a>.</li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdyuukk-l-h/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>PUBLIC SAFETY ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div
